critique.summary: The article discusses Martin Fowler's 'First Law of Distributed Object Design,' which advises against distributing objects due to the complexity and failure risks of remote calls. Fowler explains that microservices do not violate this law because they avoid in-process/remote transparency and instead use coarse-grained, document-based communication, though he acknowledges the complexity distribution adds and the ongoing debate about microservices versus monolithic architectures.
mainPoints: The First Law of Distributed Object Design states 'don't distribute your objects' because remote calls differ fundamentally from in-process calls in speed and reliability.
Distributed objects aimed for transparency between local and remote calls, which is flawed due to network unreliability and latency.
Microservices avoid this flaw by not attempting in-process/remote transparency and using coarse-grained APIs over HTTP or messaging.
Distribution adds complexity, especially in handling failures, consistency, and performance, making monolithic designs simpler by default.
Despite his reservations, Fowler supports microservices empirically because many teams, including Netflix and Amazon, have found success with them.
There is no definitive evidence favoring microservices over monoliths; both have successful examples, and the choice depends on context.
The article emphasizes clear communication of lessons learned to help readers make informed architectural decisions.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reflects Martin Fowler's well-known views on distributed objects and microservices, consistent with his published works and public talks. The distinctions between distributed objects and microservices, the challenges of remote calls, and the complexity trade-offs are widely recognized in software architecture literature. The examples cited (Netflix, Amazon, Etsy, Facebook) are commonly referenced in discussions about microservices versus monoliths. The article does not make unsupported claims and acknowledges the ongoing debate and lack of definitive evidence, which aligns with the current state of knowledge in the field.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore specific technical strategies or tools that mitigate distributed system complexity, such as service meshes or circuit breakers.
It does not address organizational or cultural factors that influence the success of microservices versus monoliths.
The article predates some recent developments in cloud-native architectures and container orchestration that impact microservice design and management.
It does not discuss the cost implications or operational overhead differences between microservices and monolithic architectures.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the practical challenges and realities of implementing microservices versus monoliths, with emphasis on complexity in inter-service communication, the role of event sourcing, and evolving asynchronous patterns. Some commenters share personal experiences and visions for future distributed system tooling.

comments.author: torginus
comments.text:
An anecdote I like to tell:

I once participated in implementing a system as a monolith, and later on handled the rewrite to microservices, to 'future-proof' the system.

The nice thing is that I have the Jira tickets for both projects, and I have actual hard proof, the microservice version absolutely didn't go smoother or take less time or dev hours.

You can really match up a lot of it feature-by-feature and it'll be plainly visible that the microservice version of the feature took longer and had more bugs.

And Imo this is the best case scenario for microservices. The 'good thing' about microservices, is once you have the interfaces, you can start coding. This makes these projects look more productive at least initially.

But the issue is that, more often than not, the quality of the specs are not great to awful, I've seen projects where basically Team A and Team B coded their service against a wildly different interface, and it was only found in the final stretch that these two parts do not meet.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a firsthand account with concrete evidence (Jira tickets) comparing monolith and microservice implementations, highlighting real-world complexity and coordination issues.

comments.author: torginus
comments.text:
> While small microservices are certainly simpler to reason about, I worry that this pushes complexity into the interconnections between services

100% true in retrospect.
comments.analysis: This comment succinctly captures a well-known tradeoff in microservices architecture, emphasizing that distributed complexity often shifts rather than disappears.

comments.author: gethly
comments.text:
99% of systems out there are not truly microservices but SOA(fat services). A microservice is something that send emails, transforms images, encodes video and so on. Most real services are 100x bigger than that.

Secondly, if you are not doing event sourcing from the get go, doing distributed system is stupid beyond imagination.

When you do event sourcing, you can do CQRS and therefore have zero need for some humongous database that scales ad infinitum and costs and arm and a leg.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly distinguishes between true microservices and large SOA services and advocates event sourcing, which is a recognized pattern for managing distributed state, though the tone is somewhat extreme.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
A lot of this first law was specifically coupled to how these systems often hid that distributed objects were distributed. In the past 10 years, async has become far more common place, and it makes the distributed boundary much less like a secret special anomaly that you wouldn't otherwise deal with and far more like just another type of async code.

I still thoroughly want to see capnproto or capnweb emerge the third party handoff, so we can do distributed systems where we tell microservice-b to use the results from microservice-a to run it's compute, without needing to proxy those results through ourself. Oh to dream.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses the evolution of asynchronous programming in distributed systems and expresses interest in emerging technologies that could improve inter-service communication.

comments.author: rednafi
comments.text:
I dream of a SQL like engine for distributed systems where you can declaratively say "svc A uses the results of B & C where C depends on D."

Then the engine would find the best way to resolve the graph and fetch the results. You could still add your imperative logic on top of the fetched results, but you don't concern yourself with the minutiae of resilience patterns and how to traverse the dependency graph.
comments.analysis: This visionary comment proposes a declarative approach to managing distributed service dependencies, which aligns with ongoing research and tooling trends, though it remains aspirational.

comments.author: Havoc
comments.text: AI has also changed the dynamics around this. Splitting things into smaller components now has a dev advantage because the AI program better with smaller scope
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a novel perspective on AI-assisted development favoring smaller components, which is plausible but less substantiated compared to other comments.
